title: Today's Tech Brief: Five Eyes: Watch out for odd LinkedIn connection requests, China's back on the hunt...
summary: A look at China's renewed efforts to gather state secrets and a security lapse in Meta's AI chatbot.

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your tech briefing. Let's dive into some interesting developments in the world of technology and security. I've got a couple of stories to share with you today that highlight the ongoing challenges in the digital world.

First off, the Five Eyes intelligence alliance is warning people to be cautious with LinkedIn connection requests. It seems China is ramping up its efforts to gather state secrets, using tactics like cash-for-intel tradecraft. This isn't new, but it's concerning to see it continue. If you get a LinkedIn request from someone you don't know, and their profile seems off, it might be a good idea to think twice before accepting. I've had a few of those requests myself, and I have to admit, I usually just ignore them.

Next, Meta is dealing with a security issue. Thousands of Instagram accounts were hijacked by hackers who abused the platform's AI chatbot. Essentially, hackers tricked the chatbot into taking control of accounts. This is a good reminder that even the most advanced technology can have vulnerabilities. It's a bit unsettling to think about, but it's a good thing Meta is on top of it.

Five Eyes: Watch out for odd LinkedIn connection requests, China's back on the hunt for state secrets is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Security Five Eyes: Watch out for odd LinkedIn connection requests, China's back on the hunt for state secrets Cash-for-intel tradecraft continues to concern intelligence officials years after it was first spotted Connor Jones Connor Jones Cybersecurity reporter Published thu 4 Jun 2026 // 12:57 UTC MI5 and its international allies are once again warning th... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

Meta confirms 1000s of Instagram accounts were hacked by abusing its AI chatbot is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Meta is notifying thousands of people whose Instagram accounts were hijacked during the months-long abuse of the company's AI chatbot, which hackers repeatedly tricked into taking control of a person's account. In a new data breach notification letter , seen by this week in security , Meta has revealed for the first time how many people had their accounts h... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
